key_insight: A brief, hybrid Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) program was feasible, acceptable, and showed early signals of improved psychological well-being for people at risk of inherited prion disease.
clinical_relevance: This supports a scalable psychosocial intervention option for individuals living with genetic neurodegenerative risk, where supportive care is currently limited.
method: 23 adults at risk of inherited prion disease completed a 5-hour group ACT workshop plus an individual booster session.
Feasibility, acceptability, and psychological outcomes were tracked at baseline, 1 month, and 3 months.
Semi-structured interviews were used to capture participant experiences and practical needs.
evidence_signal: All prespecified feasibility targets were met, and quantitative follow-up showed improvements in psychological quality of life and behavioral awareness, with stronger effects in participants with higher baseline depressive symptoms.
watch_item: This was a small feasibility study without a control group, so the findings are preliminary and need controlled testing before broader implementation.
paper_title: A Brief Acceptance and Commitment Therapy Intervention to Support Those Living At Risk of Inherited Prion Disease
